title: Quiz: Convergence Analysis
description: Test your understanding of convergence in VQAs

questions.question: What is patience-based stopping?

options.text: Waiting for the gradient to be exactly zero
Stopping after a fixed number of steps without improvement

questions.explanation: Patience-based stopping waits for a specified number of steps without improvement before concluding that optimization has converged. This is more robust to noise than checking single conditions.

questions.question: What does high variance in the loss curve typically indicate?

options.text: High shot noise or learning rate issues

questions.explanation: High variance in loss typically indicates high shot noise (too few measurement shots) or a learning rate that's too large, causing oscillation around the optimum.

questions.question: What is multi-start optimization?

options.text: Running optimization from multiple random initializations
Starting with a large learning rate
Optimizing one parameter at a time

questions.explanation: Multi-start optimization runs the optimization from multiple random initial parameter values to increase the chance of finding the global minimum rather than getting stuck in a local minimum.

questions.question: A zero gradient norm always means the optimization has found the global minimum.
questions.explanation: False. A zero gradient only indicates a stationary point, which could be a local minimum, saddle point, or maximum. In VQAs with barren plateaus, gradients can be near-zero even far from any optimum.

questions.question: What is path efficiency in trajectory analysis?

options.text: The ratio of direct distance to path length

questions.explanation: Path efficiency is the ratio of the direct Euclidean distance from start to end, divided by the total path length traveled. Low efficiency indicates a zigzag path suggesting potential for improvement with momentum.

questions.question: What should you do if multiple random starts give very different final costs?

options.text: Pick the run with the highest cost
The landscape likely has multiple local minima - investigate further
Use the average of all results

questions.explanation: Different final costs from random starts indicate the landscape has multiple local minima. You should investigate further - the lowest cost is likely best, but consider using more starts or different strategies.

questions.question: What is a sign of oscillation in optimization?

options.text: Many sign changes in cost differences

questions.explanation: Oscillation is characterized by many sign changes in the cost differences (loss going up and down repeatedly). This typically indicates the learning rate is too high.

questions.question: Which are valid strategies for escaping local minima? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Multi-start (a), perturbation (b), and simulated annealing (d) are all valid strategies for escaping local minima. Reducing learning rate to zero would stop progress entirely.
